title: The street food stall I went to every day
summary: Describe a street food stall you visited regularly and explain why you kept going back.
topic: A street food stall I visited every day
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences about a street food stall you went to regularly. Describe the food, the person who ran it, and why you always came back. Use present simple for habits and past simple for specific memories.
hintStarters: Near my office, there was a small stall that sold…
The owner always remembered my order, which made me feel…
Every time I went, I noticed that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: địa điểm yêu thích / nhận ra

sampleText: There was a small noodle stall on Sukhumvit Soi 38 in Bangkok that I went to almost every evening for three months. The owner was a woman of about sixty with short grey hair who worked alone at a single wok. She always had three things on the menu and never changed them. I ordered the pork noodle soup every time because the broth had a deep, clean flavour that I could not find anywhere else in the city. It was also a bargain. She recognised me after my second visit and always started making my order when she saw me join the queue. One evening I arrived late and the stall was already closing. She saw me from the end of the street and held up one finger, which I understood to mean she had one portion left. I ran. She was already pouring the soup into the bowl when I arrived. I ate standing up at the small counter. She washed her wok while I ate, and we did not speak, but it did not feel strange. When I finally left Bangkok, I went to that stall on my last evening and said goodbye. She nodded and gave me an extra portion of pork. I think about that soup a lot.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: worked alone at a single wok
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: làm việc một mình tại một chiếc chảo
không bao giờ thay đổi chúng
không thể tìm thấy ở nơi nào khác

sampleAnnotations.phrase: recognised me after my second visit
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nhận ra tôi sau lần thứ hai đến
bắt đầu làm món của tôi

sampleAnnotations.phrase: pouring the soup into the bowl
we did not speak, but it did not feel strange
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: chúng tôi không nói chuyện, nhưng nó không cảm thấy lạ
cho tôi thêm một khẩu phần

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I think about that soup a lot
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Tôi nghĩ về tô súp đó rất nhiều

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Adverbs of frequency with past simple
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Trạng từ tần suất như 'always', 'never', 'every time', 'almost every evening' kết hợp với past simple để mô tả thói quen trong quá khứ. Ví dụ: 'I went to almost every evening', 'She always had three things', 'She never changed them'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: Frequency adverbs like 'always', 'never', 'every time', 'almost every evening' combine with past simple to describe past habits. Examples: 'I went almost every evening', 'She always had three things', 'She never changed them'.

sampleTranslationVi: Có một sạp mì nhỏ ở Sukhumvit Soi 38 tại Bangkok mà tôi đến gần như mỗi buổi tối trong ba tháng. Chủ sạp là một người phụ nữ khoảng sáu mươi tuổi với mái tóc xám ngắn, làm việc một mình tại một chiếc chảo. Bà luôn có ba món trên thực đơn và không bao giờ thay đổi chúng. Tôi gọi món súp mì heo mỗi lần vì nước dùng có hương vị đậm đà, thanh mà tôi không thể tìm thấy ở nơi nào khác trong thành phố. Giá cũng rất hợp lý. Bà nhận ra tôi sau lần thứ hai đến và luôn bắt đầu làm món của tôi khi thấy tôi vào hàng. Một buổi tối tôi đến muộn và sạp đã sắp đóng cửa. Bà nhìn thấy tôi từ cuối phố và giơ một ngón tay lên, điều mà tôi hiểu là còn một khẩu phần. Tôi chạy. Bà đã rót súp vào bát khi tôi đến nơi. Tôi ăn đứng tại quầy nhỏ. Bà rửa chảo trong khi tôi ăn, và chúng tôi không nói chuyện, nhưng không cảm thấy lạ. Khi cuối cùng rời Bangkok, tôi đến sạp đó vào buổi tối cuối cùng và chào tạm biệt. Bà gật đầu và cho tôi thêm một khẩu phần thịt heo. Tôi nghĩ về tô súp đó rất nhiều.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that correctly uses 'never' with past simple.
options: She never change the menu.
She did never change the menu.
She never changed the menu.
She never did changed the menu.
mcQuestions.explanation: With 'never', no auxiliary 'did' is needed in affirmative past simple. 'She never changed' is the correct form.
mcQuestions.hint: Where does 'never' go in relation to the main verb?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence about a person starting to do something on seeing you.
options: She started to make my order when she seen me.
She started making my order when she sees me.
She starts making my order when she saw me.
She started making my order when she saw me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Started making' (or 'started to make') and 'saw' are both past simple, keeping the story consistent. 'She seen' is incorrect — 'seen' is the past participle, not past simple.
mcQuestions.hint: In a past story, what tense should 'see' be?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly says that something had a very good taste?
options: The broth had a deep, clean flavour.
The broth had a deep, clean taste like.
The broth was having a deep, clean flavour.
The broth has had a deep, clean flavour.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had a flavour' is past simple of 'have'. 'Was having' is incorrect — 'have' in the sense of possession is a state verb. 'Has had' is present perfect and breaks the past narrative.
mcQuestions.hint: Is 'have' (to possess a quality) an action or state verb?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the sentence where 'hold up' is used correctly to show a gesture.
options: She holded up one finger.
She held up one finger.
She hold up one finger.
She was holding up one finger.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Held' is the irregular past simple of 'hold'. 'Holded' is incorrect. 'Was holding' is continuous and changes the feel of the quick gesture.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the irregular past simple of 'hold'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence about eating while standing.
options: I ate standing to at the counter.
I ate stood up at the counter.
I ate standing up at the counter.
I ate to stand up at the counter.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Ate standing up' uses a participle phrase to describe the manner of eating. 'Stood' and 'to stand' are not the correct forms for a simultaneous action.
mcQuestions.hint: How do you describe how you ate — using a present participle or past form?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the word that means the value was very good for the price.
options: crispy
familiar
portion
bargain
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A bargain' means something is very good value for money. 'Crispy' describes texture; 'familiar' describes what you know well; 'portion' is the amount of food.
mcQuestions.hint: Which word means 'very cheap and good value'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'recognise' in the past to describe a habit?
options: She recognised me after my second visit.
She recognised me after my second visited.
She was recognising me after my second visit.
She recognised to me after my second visit.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Recognised me after my second visit' is correct. 'Recognise' is a state verb and does not use continuous forms. 'Second visited' is grammatically wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: Is 'recognise' an action or state verb? Can it take the continuous form?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the correct way to describe a single bowl of soup left.
options: She had one portion leaved.
She had one portion left.
She had left one portion.
She has one portion left.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had one portion left' (past simple of 'have' + left as adjective meaning 'remaining') is the natural phrase. 'Has' is present tense; 'leaved' is not correct.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'one portion left' mean here — is 'left' a verb or adjective?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that correctly uses 'nod' to respond without words.
options: She noded.
She node.
She nodded.
She was nodded.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Nodded' doubles the 'd' before adding '-ed' (consonant-vowel-consonant rule). 'Noded' is a misspelling.
mcQuestions.hint: When a one-syllable verb ends in consonant-vowel-consonant, what happens when you add -ed?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence that correctly says you think about something often.
options: I think to that soup a lot.
I think for that soup a lot.
I think of that soup a lot often.
I think about that soup a lot.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Think about something a lot' is natural. 'Think of' is also correct but 'a lot often' is redundant.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition goes with 'think' to mean you often remember something?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence that describes a place you cannot find elsewhere.
options: I could not find it anywhere else.
I could not find it somewhere else.
I could not find it nowhere else.
I could not find it not anywhere else.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Anywhere else' is used in negative sentences. 'Somewhere else' is used in positive sentences. Double negatives ('not…nowhere') are incorrect in standard English.
mcQuestions.hint: In a negative sentence, do you use 'somewhere' or 'anywhere'?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a food you associate with a specific place or time in your life — not because it was special, but because you had it so often that it became part of your routine? What would you feel if you tasted it again?
